dependencies.description: OSPFv3 runs entirely over IPv6 link-local addresses. Neighbors must be reachable via link-local for Hello exchange. Uses multicast FF02::5 (AllSPFRouters) and FF02::6 (AllDRouters).
dependencies.name: IPv6 link-local connectivity

dependencies.description: Interface must have IPv6 enabled ('ipv6 enable' or an IPv6 address configured) to generate a link-local address and participate in OSPFv3.
dependencies.name: IPv6 enabled on interface

dependencies.description: Hello/dead intervals, area ID, and Instance ID must match between neighbors. Unlike OSPFv2, there is no network mask check — but Instance ID must match exactly.
dependencies.name: Matching OSPF parameters

dependencies.description: OSPFv3 has no native authentication fields. Security is provided by IPsec AH or ESP applied to the OSPFv3 interface. Optional but recommended in production. Mismatched IPsec config prevents adjacency formation.
dependencies.name: IPsec for authentication

failure_modes.description: OSPFv3 neighbor remains stuck in INIT (one-way) or EXSTART (master/slave negotiation fails) indefinitely. Similar to OSPFv2 but with IPv6-specific root causes including link-local addressing issues and IPsec authentication mismatches (OSPFv3 uses IPsec AH/ESP instead of OSPF-native authentication).

resolutions.action: Verify IPv6 link-local addresses: 'show ipv6 interface' — both sides need link-local
Verify IPv6 link-local addresses: 'show ipv6 interface' — both sides need link-local
Verify IPv6 link-local addresses: 'show ipv6 interface' — both sides need link-local

Verify MTU matches: 'show interface <intf>' — check IPv6 MTU on both sides
Check IPv6 ACLs: 'show ipv6 access-list' — ensure protocol 89 is permitted
Check IPv6 ACLs: 'show ipv6 access-list' — ensure protocol 89 is permitted
Check IPv6 ACLs: 'show ipv6 access-list' — ensure protocol 89 is permitted
Verify unique Router IDs: 'show ospfv3' on both routers
Verify unique Router IDs: 'show ospfv3' on both routers
Verify unique Router IDs: 'show ospfv3' on both routers
Confirm Instance ID matches: 'show ospfv3 interface' — default 0 for IPv6

root_causes.cause: Missing or misconfigured IPv6 link-local address on interface
IPsec AH/ESP authentication mismatch (key, SPI, or transform)
MTU mismatch (IPv6 MTU differs between neighbors)
ACL or firewall blocking protocol 89 over IPv6
Duplicate Router IDs in the OSPFv3 domain
Instance ID mismatch (neighbors on different instances don't form adjacency)
IPv6 not enabled on the interface ('ipv6 enable' missing)

symptoms: show ospfv3 neighbor shows INIT or EXSTART state that does not progress
Neighbor oscillates between EXSTART and DOWN
Log messages: '%OSPFv3-5-ADJCHG: ... went from EXSTART to DOWN'
No IPv6 routes learned from the neighbor

failure_modes.description: OSPFv3 adjacency reaches FULL state but no IPv6 (or IPv4) routes are learned. The address-family model in OSPFv3 requires explicit activation of each address family. Without proper AF configuration, the LSDB is synchronized but routes are not installed.

resolutions.action: Verify address-family configuration: 'show running-config | section ospfv3'
Verify address-family configuration: 'show running-config | section ospfv3'
Verify address-family configuration: 'show running-config | section ospfv3'
Ensure 'address-family ipv6 unicast' (or ipv4) is configured under the OSPFv3 process
Ensure 'address-family ipv6 unicast' (or ipv4) is configured under the OSPFv3 process
Ensure 'address-family ipv6 unicast' (or ipv4) is configured under the OSPFv3 process
Activate interface under the correct AF: 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6' (NX-OS)
Activate interface under the correct AF: 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6' (NX-OS)
Activate interface under the correct AF: 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6' (NX-OS)
Check Instance ID: 0 for IPv6 (default), 64 for IPv4 unicast on NX-OS
Check Instance ID: 0 for IPv6 (default), 64 for IPv4 unicast on NX-OS
Check Instance ID: 0 for IPv6 (default), 64 for IPv4 unicast on NX-OS
Verify routes: 'show ipv6 route ospf' and 'show ospfv3 database' to compare LSDB vs. RIB

root_causes.cause: Address-family ipv6 unicast not configured under OSPFv3 process
Address-family ipv4 unicast not configured when expecting IPv4-over-OSPFv3
Wrong Instance ID for the address family (e.g., instance 0 for IPv6, 64 for IPv4)
Interface not activated under the correct address-family

symptoms: OSPFv3 neighbor shows FULL state but no routes learned
'show ipv6 route ospf' or 'show ip route ospfv3' is empty despite FULL adjacency
LSDB appears synchronized but routing table missing expected prefixes
Address-family not visible under OSPFv3 process configuration

failure_modes.description: OSPFv3 uses Instance IDs (0-255) to allow multiple protocol instances per link. Neighbors with different Instance IDs on the same link will not see each other at all — Hellos are silently ignored because Instance ID is checked before neighbor processing. This is a common misconfiguration when mixing dual-stack and IPv6-only deployments.

resolutions.action: Check Instance ID on both sides: 'show ospfv3 interface <intf>'
Check Instance ID on both sides: 'show ospfv3 interface <intf>'
Check Instance ID on both sides: 'show ospfv3 interface <intf>'
Align Instance IDs: both neighbors must use the same Instance ID per AF
Align Instance IDs: both neighbors must use the same Instance ID per AF
Align Instance IDs: both neighbors must use the same Instance ID per AF
Default Instance IDs: 0 for IPv6, 64 for IPv4 unicast, 96 for IPv4 multicast
Default Instance IDs: 0 for IPv6, 64 for IPv4 unicast, 96 for IPv4 multicast
Default Instance IDs: 0 for IPv6, 64 for IPv4 unicast, 96 for IPv4 multicast
On NX-OS: Instance ID set via 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6' (uses 0) vs 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv4' (uses 64)
On NX-OS: Instance ID set via 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6' (uses 0) vs 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv4' (uses 64)
On NX-OS: Instance ID set via 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6' (uses 0) vs 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv4' (uses 64)
Packet capture on the link filtering protocol 89 to compare Instance IDs in Hellos
Packet capture on the link filtering protocol 89 to compare Instance IDs in Hellos
Packet capture on the link filtering protocol 89 to compare Instance IDs in Hellos

root_causes.cause: Instance ID explicitly set to different values on each end
One side using IPv6 instance (default 0), other side using IPv4 instance (64)
Mixed vendor/OS defaults for Instance ID
Migrating from single-AF to dual-stack without aligning Instance IDs

show ospfv3 neighbor is empty for the expected interface
Both routers have OSPFv3 enabled but don't see each other
Packet captures show Hellos being sent but with different Instance IDs

messages.description: Discovers neighbors, negotiates parameters, elects DR/BDR. Sent to FF02::5 (AllSPFRouters).
key_fields: Options (V6, E, N, MC, R, DC, AF)
messages.name: Hello

messages.description: Exchanges LSDB summary during adjacency formation. Carries LSA headers describing the sender's database.
messages.name: Database Description (DBD)

messages.description: Requests specific LSAs identified as missing or newer during DBD exchange
messages.name: Link State Request (LSR)
messages.when_sent: After DBD exchange when missing/newer LSAs are identified

messages.description: Carries one or more LSAs — the core flooding mechanism. OSPFv3 uses different LSA types than OSPFv2.
key_fields: LSA Type (0x2001 Router, 0x2002 Network, 0x2003 Inter-Area-Prefix, 0x2004 Inter-Area-Router, 0x4005 AS-External, 0x2007 NSSA, 0x0008 Link, 0x2009 Intra-Area-Prefix)
messages.name: Link State Update (LSU)
messages.when_sent: In response to LSR, or when an LSA originates/changes

messages.description: Acknowledges received LSUs for reliable flooding
messages.name: Link State Acknowledgment (LSAck)

purpose: Open Shortest Path First version 3 — link-state IGP for IPv6 (and IPv4 via address families)

states.description: No Hello received from neighbor. Initial state for all neighbors. The local router has no information about the neighbor or the neighbor's dead timer has expired.
states.name: DOWN

states.description: (NBMA only) Unicast Hello sent to a manually configured neighbor, but no Hello has been received in reply. Rare in OSPFv3 deployments since NBMA is uncommon with IPv6.
states.name: ATTEMPT

states.description: Hello received from neighbor but own Router ID is not listed in the neighbor's Hello packet (one-way communication). Stuck in INIT indicates return Hellos are not reaching the neighbor — common causes include IPv6 link-local issues, ACLs blocking protocol 89 over IPv6, or IPsec authentication mismatch.
states.name: INIT

states.description: Bidirectional communication established — own Router ID has been seen in the neighbor's Hello. DR/BDR election occurs in this state on broadcast and NBMA networks. This is the steady state for DROther-to-DROther adjacencies on multi-access segments.
states.name: 2-WAY

states.description: Master/slave negotiation for Database Description (DBD) exchange. The router with the higher Router ID becomes master. Stuck in EXSTART typically indicates MTU mismatch (IPv6 MTU), IPsec authentication failure, or duplicate Router IDs.
states.name: EXSTART

states.description: Database Description packets are being exchanged. Each DBD contains LSA headers summarizing the sender's LSDB. The receiver builds a list of LSAs it needs to request.
states.name: EXCHANGE

states.description: Link State Requests (LSRs) have been sent for LSAs identified during EXCHANGE. Waiting for Link State Updates (LSUs) in response. Brief transient state.
states.name: LOADING

states.description: Fully adjacent — LSDBs are synchronized between neighbors. This is the normal steady state for DR/BDR adjacencies, point-to-point links, and virtual links. IPv6 (and optionally IPv4) routes are computed from the synchronized LSDB.
states.name: FULL

timers.description: Interval between Hello packets on OSPFv3 interfaces
timers.name: hello-interval
timers.tuning_advice: Must match all neighbors on a segment; lower for faster detection but more overhead. Consider BFD for sub-second detection instead of aggressive hello timers.

timers.description: Time without a Hello before declaring neighbor down
timers.name: dead-interval
timers.on_expiry: Neighbor declared dead; adjacency torn down; routes withdrawn and SPF recalculated
timers.tuning_advice: Must match all neighbors on a segment; consider BFD for sub-second detection instead of reducing dead-interval

timers.name: retransmit-interval
timers.tuning_advice: Increase on high-latency links; decrease on low-latency links for faster convergence

timers.description: Interval to re-originate self-originated LSAs to prevent aging out
timers.name: LSA refresh
timers.on_expiry: LSA refreshed with incremented sequence number and flooded

timers.description: Maximum age before LSA is purged from LSDB
timers.name: LSA max-age
timers.on_expiry: LSA purged from LSDB and flooded with max-age to all neighbors

timers.description: Delay before running SPF calculation after a topology change (initial-delay/hold-time/max-wait)
timers.name: SPF delay
timers.on_expiry: SPF algorithm runs, routing table updated

os_defaults.notes: IOS-XE uses conservative defaults; tune lower for faster convergence
IOS-XR uses aggressive defaults out of the box

timers.tuning_advice: Tune SPF timers (initial-delay, hold-time, max-wait) for faster convergence. Aggressive settings: 1ms/5ms/5000ms. OS defaults vary significantly.

timers.description: Throttle for LSA origination to prevent flooding storms (start-interval/hold-interval/max-interval)
timers.name: LSA throttle
timers.on_expiry: LSA can be re-originated after throttle expires
timers.tuning_advice: Tune for faster convergence in large networks; reduce start-interval for first LSA, but keep max reasonable to prevent storms

transitions.trigger: Hello received from neighbor (over IPv6 link-local)

transitions.failure_if_stuck: One-way communication — IPv6 ACL blocking protocol 89, missing link-local address, or IPsec SA mismatch
transitions.trigger: Own Router ID seen in neighbor's Hello (bidirectional communication confirmed)
Adjacency decision — at least one side is DR/BDR, or point-to-point/virtual link

transitions.failure_if_stuck: MTU mismatch (IPv6 MTU), IPsec authentication failure, or duplicate Router IDs
transitions.trigger: Master/slave agreed, DD sequence numbers synchronized
All DBDs exchanged, LSRs needed for missing LSAs
All DBDs exchanged, no LSRs needed (databases already in sync)
All requested LSAs received and acknowledged
Dead timer expired — no Hellos received from neighbor

transitions.failure_if_stuck: MTU mismatch or IPsec authentication failure preventing DBD exchange — adjacency cycles EXSTART -> DOWN repeatedly
One-way communication — IPv6 ACL, missing link-local, or asymmetric routing blocking return Hellos

transitions.trigger: Received Hello without own Router ID (neighbor restarted or misconfiguration)
(NBMA only) Neighbor manually configured, unicast Hello sent over IPv6

transport: IP protocol 89 over IPv6, multicast FF02::5 (AllSPFRouters) and FF02::6 (AllDRouters)

config_examples:
router ospfv3 1
 address-family ipv6 unicast
  area 0
interface GigabitEthernet1
 ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
 ospfv3 1 ipv6 area 0

notes.note: OSPFv3 supports address-family model: 'router ospfv3 <process>' with 'address-family ipv6/ipv4 unicast'. Also supports legacy 'ipv6 router ospf' syntax.

config_examples:
router ospfv3 1
 area 0
  interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
 address-family ipv6 unicast
  area 0
   interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0

notes.note: OSPFv3 configured under 'router ospfv3 <process>'. Supports both IPv6 and IPv4 unicast address families. Uses IPsec for authentication natively.

config_examples:
feature ospfv3
router ospfv3 1
  address-family ipv6 unicast
interface Ethernet1/1
  ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
  ospfv3 1 area 0 ipv6

notes.note: OSPFv3 requires 'feature ospfv3'. Uses 'ospfv3 <process> area <area> ipv6/ipv4' under interface. Supports address-family model for dual-stack (IPv4+IPv6 in single OSPFv3 instance).
NX-OS OSPFv3 instance ID defaults: 0 for IPv6, 64 for IPv4 unicast. Changing instance ID breaks adjacency with neighbors using different IDs.
